The term refers to the heavenly and earthly manifestations of the benevolent [[Shinto]] divinity of water (mainly [[:en:freshwater|freshwater]]), as well as to a wide variety of mythical and magical creatures found in {{ill|lake|en|lake|WD=Q23397|lt=lake}}s, {{ill|Pond|en|Pond|WD=Q12719646|lt=Pond}}s, {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs}}, and {{ill|Well|en|Well|WD=Q43483|lt=Well}}s, including serpents ({{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}}s, {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon}}s, {{ill|eel|en|eel|WD=Q128685|lt=eel}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish}}, {{ill|Turtle|en|Turtle|WD=Q223044|lt=Turtle}}s), and the flesh-eating {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=kappa}}. '''Mizu-no-kamisama''', '''Mizugami''', or '''Suijin''' is popularly {{ill|Deference|en|Deference|WD=Q396568|lt=revered}} and {{ill|Worship|en|Worship|WD=Q2514663|lt=worshipped}} in {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=temples}} and continues to influence {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese culture}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Japanese Culture: Everything you need to know {{!}} InsideJapan Tours|url=https://www.insidejapantours.com/japanese-culture/|access-date=2021-01-22|website=www.insidejapantours.com}}</ref> Other names of Suijin include {{nihongo|'''Suiten'''|水天||{{small|lit.}} "water heaven"}} and {{nihongo|'''Suiō'''|水王||{{small|lit.}} "water lord/king"}}.
Suijin is often conflated with [[Ryūjin]], the Japanese dragon god associated with water. [[Acala]] is sometimes called "Suijin" because of his believed association with {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=waterfalls}}. Suijin appears as a stone plaque or even a small stone set upright near the emergence of a spring.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=In Handbooks of World Mythology. Santa Barbara, Calif : ABC-CLIO.|isbn=9781576074671|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255 255]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255}}</ref>
==Worship==
The Shinto water god is believed to be the guardian of {{ill|Fisherman|en|Fisherman|WD=Q331432|lt=fishermen}} and the {{ill|Patron saint|en|Patron saint|WD=Q28914|lt=Patron saint}} of {{ill|Fertility|en|Fertility|WD=Q964401|lt=Fertility}}, {{ill|motherhood|en|motherhood|WD=Q1956046|lt=motherhood}}, and painless {{ill|Childbirth|en|Childbirth|WD=Q34581|lt=Childbirth}}. People worship Suijin with offerings, believing that doing so will ensure pure and unpolluted water for drinking, {{ill|Agriculture|en|Agriculture|WD=Q11451|lt=Agriculture}}, and {{ill|sanitation|en|sanitation|WD=Q949149|lt=sanitation}}, and will bring success in fishing trips, fertility, motherhood, and easy childbirth.<ref name=Schumacher>{{cite web|last=Schumacher|first=Mark|title=Suijin|url=http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html|publisher=www.onmarkproductions.com|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> [[Shrine]]s devoted to the worship of the Water God are called Suitengū Shrines.
There are several numbers of shrines in Japan associated with deities. An example of this is the Horse God in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka}}, the main shrine for all Suitengū Shrines in Japan. This shrine is visited by pregnant women who wish to ensure a safe and easy birth for their children. Another famous Suitengū Shrine is the {{ill|Tsukiji|en|Tsukiji|WD=Q1201337|lt=Tsukiji}} [[Suitengū (Tokyo)|Suijin Shrine]], located near the {{ill|Tsukiji fish market|en|Tsukiji fish market|WD=Q859471|lt=Tsukiji fish market}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, which was built to protect and watch over the fishermen and their businesses.<ref name=Schumacher />
Suijin is widely worshipped at Suitengū Shrines throughout Japan through {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=votive stone markers}} devoted to the Water God. Most of these stone markers can be found enshrined at {{ill|ditch|en|ditch|WD=Q2048319|lt=dikes}}, agricultural irrigation canals, [[:en:Rice paddy|Rice paddy]] fields, mountain {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs}}, regular springs, streams, rivers, wells, household wells, and even inside sewage water and [[:en:septic tanks|septic tanks]]. When a Suijin stone marker is enshrined at mountain springs that form sources for agricultural waterways, it is often labelled as "water distributing god" (kumari no kami 水分神), in which case it may also be associated with the god of the mountain (Yama no kami 山の神).<ref name=Schumacher />
=== Festivals ===
There are many Japanese festivals dedicated to the water god, most of which occur during summer and winter, though more emphasis is placed on summer festivals. The majority of summer festivals occur at the [[Yasaka Shrine]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and the [[Tsushima Shrine]] in {{ill|Tsushima, Aichi|en|Tsushima, Aichi|WD=Q816736|lt=Tsushima}}. These observances have a significant role in the {{ill|Exorcism|en|Exorcism|WD=Q172663|lt=Exorcism}}<ref>Yen, A. (1975). "Shang-ssu" Festival and Its Myths in China and Japan. Asian Folklore Studies, 34(2), 45-85. doi:10.2307/1177626</ref> of bad spirits and purification, primarily aimed at dangerous {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic}}s, {{ill|Disease|en|Disease|WD=Q12136|lt=Disease}}s and {{ill|Natural disaster|en|Natural disaster|WD=Q8065|lt=natural and man-made water-related disasters}} which commonly happen during the {{ill|summer|en|summer|WD=Q1313|lt=summer}}.
List of festivals worshipping Suijin (this list may not include all festivals):<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi>{{cite web|last=Higashi|title=Suijin|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=227|publisher=Kokugakuin University|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> * Suijin [[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]], December 1 and June 15 — On these days in Japan, various locations hold the [https://www.japanallover.com/2006/05/suijin-matsuri/ Suijin Matsuri], a Shinto celebration and ceremony to honour the god of water. * Kamakura Matsuri, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}, around February 15–17 — {{ill|Igloo|en|Igloo|WD=Q101805|lt=Igloo}}s (called "Kamakura") are built with a small alcove inside dedicated to Suijin, who is honoured during this festival to ensure good crops in the coming year. This festival is over 400 years old. * Suijin-sai, {{ill|Funabashi|en|Funabashi|WD=Q271417|lt=Funabashi}} Fishing Harbour ({{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba}}), April 3 — A festival to pray for good fishing hauls and safety at sea. All [[:en:fishing boats|fishing boats]] from the area gather in the harbour and a [[Kagura|Kagura ritual]] (sacred Shinto music and dance), dedicated to the god of the sea, is performed onboard each vessel. A ritual existing since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo Period}}, the festival is called Futuna-sai for short. * Suitengū Spring Festival, Kurume City ({{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka}}), May 3–5 — This annual festival, held at the Suitengū Shrine in Kurume City, is reportedly over 800 years old. Participants pray for safe and easy childbirth, for protection from {{ill|Drowning|en|Drowning|WD=Q506616|lt=Drowning}}, and ask for the safety and health of children. This festival is also held at other Suitengū sites in Japan on May 5. Another major festival in Kurume City is the Water Festival, which takes place from August 3 to 5. * Okinohata Suitengu Festival, {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa}} (Fukuoka), May 3–5 — Held on the canal by the shrine for three days and nights, drawing about 30,000 total visitors. Many pray for their children to be protected from drowning. * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]], near {{ill|Tsukuba|en|Tsukuba|WD=Q273863|lt=Tsukuba}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki}}), around July 25 — The Anniversary feast of Suijin-gū Shrine of Tsuchiura City (Ibaraki). Water is splashed over the portable shrine.<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] {{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3137449]] Wikidata name: Suijin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto god of water in Japan}}[[File:水神.JPG|thumb|An altar to Suijin, located along the {{ill|Kiso River|en|Kiso River|WD=Q779607|lt=Kiso River}}]] {{nihongo|'''Suijin''' or '''Mizugami'''|, すいじん, みずがみ||{{small|lit.}} "water god/deity"}} is a general name for the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=god of water}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. The term refers to the heavenly and earthly manifestations of the benevolent [[Shinto]] divinity of water (mainly [[:en:freshwater|freshwater]]), as well as to a wide variety of mythical and magical creatures found in {{ill|lake|en|lake|WD=Q23397|lt=lake}}s, {{ill|Pond|en|Pond|WD=Q12719646|lt=Pond}}s, {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs}}, and {{ill|Well|en|Well|WD=Q43483|lt=Well}}s, including serpents ({{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}}s, {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon}}s, {{ill|eel|en|eel|WD=Q128685|lt=eel}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish}}, {{ill|Turtle|en|Turtle|WD=Q223044|lt=Turtle}}s), and the flesh-eating {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=kappa}}. '''Mizu-no-kamisama''', '''Mizugami''', or '''Suijin''' is popularly {{ill|Deference|en|Deference|WD=Q396568|lt=revered}} and {{ill|Worship|en|Worship|WD=Q2514663|lt=worshipped}} in {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=temples}} and continues to influence {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese culture}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Japanese Culture: Everything you need to know {{!}} InsideJapan Tours|url=https://www.insidejapantours.com/japanese-culture/|access-date=2021-01-22|website=www.insidejapantours.com}}</ref> Other names of Suijin include {{nihongo|'''Suiten'''|水天||{{small|lit.}} "water heaven"}} and {{nihongo|'''Suiō'''|水王||{{small|lit.}} "water lord/king"}}.
Suijin is often conflated with [[Ryūjin]], the Japanese dragon god associated with water. [[Acala]] is sometimes called "Suijin" because of his believed association with {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=waterfalls}}. Suijin appears as a stone plaque or even a small stone set upright near the emergence of a spring.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=In Handbooks of World Mythology. Santa Barbara, Calif : ABC-CLIO.|isbn=9781576074671|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255 255]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255}}</ref>
==Worship==
The Shinto water god is believed to be the guardian of {{ill|Fisherman|en|Fisherman|WD=Q331432|lt=fishermen}} and the {{ill|Patron saint|en|Patron saint|WD=Q28914|lt=Patron saint}} of {{ill|Fertility|en|Fertility|WD=Q964401|lt=Fertility}}, {{ill|motherhood|en|motherhood|WD=Q1956046|lt=motherhood}}, and painless {{ill|Childbirth|en|Childbirth|WD=Q34581|lt=Childbirth}}. People worship Suijin with offerings, believing that doing so will ensure pure and unpolluted water for drinking, {{ill|Agriculture|en|Agriculture|WD=Q11451|lt=Agriculture}}, and {{ill|sanitation|en|sanitation|WD=Q949149|lt=sanitation}}, and will bring success in fishing trips, fertility, motherhood, and easy childbirth.<ref name=Schumacher>{{cite web|last=Schumacher|first=Mark|title=Suijin|url=http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html|publisher=www.onmarkproductions.com|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> [[Shrine]]s devoted to the worship of the Water God are called Suitengū Shrines.
There are several numbers of shrines in Japan associated with deities. An example of this is the Horse God in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka}}, the main shrine for all Suitengū Shrines in Japan. This shrine is visited by pregnant women who wish to ensure a safe and easy birth for their children. Another famous Suitengū Shrine is the {{ill|Tsukiji|en|Tsukiji|WD=Q1201337|lt=Tsukiji}} [[Suitengū (Tokyo)|Suijin Shrine]], located near the {{ill|Tsukiji fish market|en|Tsukiji fish market|WD=Q859471|lt=Tsukiji fish market}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, which was built to protect and watch over the fishermen and their businesses.<ref name=Schumacher />
Suijin is widely worshipped at Suitengū Shrines throughout Japan through {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=votive stone markers}} devoted to the Water God. Most of these stone markers can be found enshrined at {{ill|ditch|en|ditch|WD=Q2048319|lt=dikes}}, agricultural irrigation canals, [[:en:Rice paddy|Rice paddy]] fields, mountain {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs}}, regular springs, streams, rivers, wells, household wells, and even inside sewage water and [[:en:septic tanks|septic tanks]]. When a Suijin stone marker is enshrined at mountain springs that form sources for agricultural waterways, it is often labelled as "water distributing god" (kumari no kami 水分神), in which case it may also be associated with the god of the mountain (Yama no kami 山の神).<ref name=Schumacher />
=== Festivals ===
There are many Japanese festivals dedicated to the water god, most of which occur during summer and winter, though more emphasis is placed on summer festivals. The majority of summer festivals occur at the [[Yasaka Shrine]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and the [[Tsushima Shrine]] in {{ill|Tsushima, Aichi|en|Tsushima, Aichi|WD=Q816736|lt=Tsushima}}. These observances have a significant role in the {{ill|Exorcism|en|Exorcism|WD=Q172663|lt=Exorcism}}<ref>Yen, A. (1975). "Shang-ssu" Festival and Its Myths in China and Japan. Asian Folklore Studies, 34(2), 45-85. doi:10.2307/1177626</ref> of bad spirits and purification, primarily aimed at dangerous {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic}}s, {{ill|Disease|en|Disease|WD=Q12136|lt=Disease}}s and {{ill|Natural disaster|en|Natural disaster|WD=Q8065|lt=natural and man-made water-related disasters}} which commonly happen during the {{ill|summer|en|summer|WD=Q1313|lt=summer}}.
List of festivals worshipping Suijin (this list may not include all festivals):<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi>{{cite web|last=Higashi|title=Suijin|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=227|publisher=Kokugakuin University|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> * Suijin [[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]], December 1 and June 15 — On these days in Japan, various locations hold the [https://www.japanallover.com/2006/05/suijin-matsuri/ Suijin Matsuri], a Shinto celebration and ceremony to honour the god of water. * Kamakura Matsuri, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}, around February 15–17 — {{ill|Igloo|en|Igloo|WD=Q101805|lt=Igloo}}s (called "Kamakura") are built with a small alcove inside dedicated to Suijin, who is honoured during this festival to ensure good crops in the coming year. This festival is over 400 years old. * Suijin-sai, {{ill|Funabashi|en|Funabashi|WD=Q271417|lt=Funabashi}} Fishing Harbour ({{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba}}), April 3 — A festival to pray for good fishing hauls and safety at sea. All [[:en:fishing boats|fishing boats]] from the area gather in the harbour and a [[Kagura|Kagura ritual]] (sacred Shinto music and dance), dedicated to the god of the sea, is performed onboard each vessel. A ritual existing since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo Period}}, the festival is called Futuna-sai for short. * Suitengū Spring Festival, Kurume City ({{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka}}), May 3–5 — This annual festival, held at the Suitengū Shrine in Kurume City, is reportedly over 800 years old. Participants pray for safe and easy childbirth, for protection from {{ill|Drowning|en|Drowning|WD=Q506616|lt=Drowning}}, and ask for the safety and health of children. This festival is also held at other Suitengū sites in Japan on May 5. Another major festival in Kurume City is the Water Festival, which takes place from August 3 to 5. * Okinohata Suitengu Festival, {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa}} (Fukuoka), May 3–5 — Held on the canal by the shrine for three days and nights, drawing about 30,000 total visitors. Many pray for their children to be protected from drowning. * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]], near {{ill|Tsukuba|en|Tsukuba|WD=Q273863|lt=Tsukuba}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki}}), around July 25 — The Anniversary feast of Suijin-gū Shrine of Tsuchiura City (Ibaraki). Water is splashed over the portable shrine.<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi />
== References == {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://archive.today/20130210114439/http://groups.yahoo.com/group/Darumasan-Japan/message/496 Yahoo groups] *[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/12-devas.shtml On Mark Productions: Twelve Devas] *[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html On Mark Productions: Suijin]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] {{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3137449]] Wikidata name: Suijin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto god of water in Japan}}[[File:水神.JPG|thumb|An altar to Suijin, located along the {{ill|Kiso River|en|Kiso River|WD=Q779607|lt=Kiso River}}]] {{nihongo|'''Suijin''' or '''Mizugami'''|, すいじん, みずがみ||{{small|lit.}} "water god/deity"}} is a general name for the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=god of water}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. The term refers to the heavenly and earthly manifestations of the benevolent [[Shinto]] divinity of water (mainly [[:en:freshwater|freshwater]]), as well as to a wide variety of mythical and magical creatures found in {{ill|lake|en|lake|WD=Q23397|lt=lake}}s, {{ill|Pond|en|Pond|WD=Q12719646|lt=Pond}}s, {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs}}, and {{ill|Well|en|Well|WD=Q43483|lt=Well}}s, including serpents ({{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}}s, {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon}}s, {{ill|eel|en|eel|WD=Q128685|lt=eel}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish}}, {{ill|Turtle|en|Turtle|WD=Q223044|lt=Turtle}}s), and the flesh-eating {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=kappa}}. '''Mizu-no-kamisama''', '''Mizugami''', or '''Suijin''' is popularly {{ill|Deference|en|Deference|WD=Q396568|lt=revered}} and {{ill|Worship|en|Worship|WD=Q2514663|lt=worshipped}} in {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=temples}} and continues to influence {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese culture}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Japanese Culture: Everything you need to know {{!}} InsideJapan Tours|url=https://www.insidejapantours.com/japanese-culture/|access-date=2021-01-22|website=www.insidejapantours.com}}</ref> Other names of Suijin include {{nihongo|'''Suiten'''|水天||{{small|lit.}} "water heaven"}} and {{nihongo|'''Suiō'''|水王||{{small|lit.}} "water lord/king"}}.
Suijin is often conflated with [[Ryūjin]], the Japanese dragon god associated with water. [[Acala]] is sometimes called "Suijin" because of his believed association with {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=waterfalls}}. Suijin appears as a stone plaque or even a small stone set upright near the emergence of a spring.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=In Handbooks of World Mythology. Santa Barbara, Calif : ABC-CLIO.|isbn=9781576074671|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255 255]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255}}</ref>
==Worship==
The Shinto water god is believed to be the guardian of {{ill|Fisherman|en|Fisherman|WD=Q331432|lt=fishermen}} and the {{ill|Patron saint|en|Patron saint|WD=Q28914|lt=Patron saint}} of {{ill|Fertility|en|Fertility|WD=Q964401|lt=Fertility}}, {{ill|motherhood|en|motherhood|WD=Q1956046|lt=motherhood}}, and painless {{ill|Childbirth|en|Childbirth|WD=Q34581|lt=Childbirth}}. People worship Suijin with offerings, believing that doing so will ensure pure and unpolluted water for drinking, {{ill|Agriculture|en|Agriculture|WD=Q11451|lt=Agriculture}}, and {{ill|sanitation|en|sanitation|WD=Q949149|lt=sanitation}}, and will bring success in fishing trips, fertility, motherhood, and easy childbirth.<ref name=Schumacher>{{cite web|last=Schumacher|first=Mark|title=Suijin|url=http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html|publisher=www.onmarkproductions.com|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> [[Shrine]]s devoted to the worship of the Water God are called Suitengū Shrines.
There are several numbers of shrines in Japan associated with deities. An example of this is the Horse God in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka}}, the main shrine for all Suitengū Shrines in Japan. This shrine is visited by pregnant women who wish to ensure a safe and easy birth for their children. Another famous Suitengū Shrine is the {{ill|Tsukiji|en|Tsukiji|WD=Q1201337|lt=Tsukiji}} [[Suitengū (Tokyo)|Suijin Shrine]], located near the {{ill|Tsukiji fish market|en|Tsukiji fish market|WD=Q859471|lt=Tsukiji fish market}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, which was built to protect and watch over the fishermen and their businesses.<ref name=Schumacher />
Suijin is widely worshipped at Suitengū Shrines throughout Japan through {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=votive stone markers}} devoted to the Water God. Most of these stone markers can be found enshrined at {{ill|ditch|en|ditch|WD=Q2048319|lt=dikes}}, agricultural irrigation canals, [[:en:Rice paddy|Rice paddy]] fields, mountain {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs}}, regular springs, streams, rivers, wells, household wells, and even inside sewage water and [[:en:septic tanks|septic tanks]]. When a Suijin stone marker is enshrined at mountain springs that form sources for agricultural waterways, it is often labelled as "water distributing god" (kumari no kami 水分神), in which case it may also be associated with the god of the mountain (Yama no kami 山の神).<ref name=Schumacher />
=== Festivals ===
There are many Japanese festivals dedicated to the water god, most of which occur during summer and winter, though more emphasis is placed on summer festivals. The majority of summer festivals occur at the [[Yasaka Shrine]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and the [[Tsushima Shrine]] in {{ill|Tsushima, Aichi|en|Tsushima, Aichi|WD=Q816736|lt=Tsushima}}. These observances have a significant role in the {{ill|Exorcism|en|Exorcism|WD=Q172663|lt=Exorcism}}<ref>Yen, A. (1975). "Shang-ssu" Festival and Its Myths in China and Japan. Asian Folklore Studies, 34(2), 45-85. doi:10.2307/1177626</ref> of bad spirits and purification, primarily aimed at dangerous {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic}}s, {{ill|Disease|en|Disease|WD=Q12136|lt=Disease}}s and {{ill|Natural disaster|en|Natural disaster|WD=Q8065|lt=natural and man-made water-related disasters}} which commonly happen during the {{ill|summer|en|summer|WD=Q1313|lt=summer}}.
List of festivals worshipping Suijin (this list may not include all festivals):<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi>{{cite web|last=Higashi|title=Suijin|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=227|publisher=Kokugakuin University|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> * Suijin [[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]], December 1 and June 15 — On these days in Japan, various locations hold the [https://www.japanallover.com/2006/05/suijin-matsuri/ Suijin Matsuri], a Shinto celebration and ceremony to honour the god of water. * Kamakura Matsuri, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}, around February 15–17 — {{ill|Igloo|en|Igloo|WD=Q101805|lt=Igloo}}s (called "Kamakura") are built with a small alcove inside dedicated to Suijin, who is honoured during this festival to ensure good crops in the coming year. This festival is over 400 years old. * Suijin-sai, {{ill|Funabashi|en|Funabashi|WD=Q271417|lt=Funabashi}} Fishing Harbour ({{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba}}), April 3 — A festival to pray for good fishing hauls and safety at sea. All [[:en:fishing boats|fishing boats]] from the area gather in the harbour and a [[Kagura|Kagura ritual]] (sacred Shinto music and dance), dedicated to the god of the sea, is performed onboard each vessel. A ritual existing since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo Period}}, the festival is called Futuna-sai for short. * Suitengū Spring Festival, Kurume City ({{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka}}), May 3–5 — This annual festival, held at the Suitengū Shrine in Kurume City, is reportedly over 800 years old. Participants pray for safe and easy childbirth, for protection from {{ill|Drowning|en|Drowning|WD=Q506616|lt=Drowning}}, and ask for the safety and health of children. This festival is also held at other Suitengū sites in Japan on May 5. Another major festival in Kurume City is the Water Festival, which takes place from August 3 to 5. * Okinohata Suitengu Festival, {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa}} (Fukuoka), May 3–5 — Held on the canal by the shrine for three days and nights, drawing about 30,000 total visitors. Many pray for their children to be protected from drowning. * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]], near {{ill|Tsukuba|en|Tsukuba|WD=Q273863|lt=Tsukuba}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki}}), around July 25 — The Anniversary feast of Suijin-gū Shrine of Tsuchiura City (Ibaraki). Water is splashed over the portable shrine.<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi />
== References == {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://archive.today/20130210114439/http://groups.yahoo.com/group/Darumasan-Japan/message/496 Yahoo groups] *[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/12-devas.shtml On Mark Productions: Twelve Devas] *[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html On Mark Productions: Suijin]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] {{Infobox deity}} {{Short description|Shinto god of water in Japan}}[[File:水神.JPG|thumb|An altar to Suijin, located along the {{ill|Kiso River|en|Kiso River|WD=Q779607|lt=Kiso River}}]] {{nihongo|'''Suijin''' or '''Mizugami'''|, すいじん, みずがみ||{{small|lit.}} "water god/deity"}} is a general name for the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=god of water}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. The term refers to the heavenly and earthly manifestations of the benevolent [[Shinto]] divinity of water (mainly [[:en:freshwater|freshwater]]), as well as to a wide variety of mythical and magical creatures found in {{ill|lake|en|lake|WD=Q23397|lt=lake}}s, {{ill|Pond|en|Pond|WD=Q12719646|lt=Pond}}s, {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs}}, and {{ill|Well|en|Well|WD=Q43483|lt=Well}}s, including serpents ({{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}}s, {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon}}s, {{ill|eel|en|eel|WD=Q128685|lt=eel}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish}}, {{ill|Turtle|en|Turtle|WD=Q223044|lt=Turtle}}s), and the flesh-eating {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=kappa}}. '''Mizu-no-kamisama''', '''Mizugami''', or '''Suijin''' is popularly {{ill|Deference|en|Deference|WD=Q396568|lt=revered}} and {{ill|Worship|en|Worship|WD=Q2514663|lt=worshipped}} in {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=temples}} and continues to influence {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese culture}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Japanese Culture: Everything you need to know {{!}} InsideJapan Tours|url=https://www.insidejapantours.com/japanese-culture/|access-date=2021-01-22|website=www.insidejapantours.com}}</ref> Other names of Suijin include {{nihongo|'''Suiten'''|水天||{{small|lit.}} "water heaven"}} and {{nihongo|'''Suiō'''|水王||{{small|lit.}} "water lord/king"}}.
Suijin is often conflated with [[Ryūjin]], the Japanese dragon god associated with water. [[Acala]] is sometimes called "Suijin" because of his believed association with {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=waterfalls}}. Suijin appears as a stone plaque or even a small stone set upright near the emergence of a spring.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=In Handbooks of World Mythology. Santa Barbara, Calif : ABC-CLIO.|isbn=9781576074671|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255 255]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255}}</ref>
==Worship==
The Shinto water god is believed to be the guardian of {{ill|Fisherman|en|Fisherman|WD=Q331432|lt=fishermen}} and the {{ill|Patron saint|en|Patron saint|WD=Q28914|lt=Patron saint}} of {{ill|Fertility|en|Fertility|WD=Q964401|lt=Fertility}}, {{ill|motherhood|en|motherhood|WD=Q1956046|lt=motherhood}}, and painless {{ill|Childbirth|en|Childbirth|WD=Q34581|lt=Childbirth}}. People worship Suijin with offerings, believing that doing so will ensure pure and unpolluted water for drinking, {{ill|Agriculture|en|Agriculture|WD=Q11451|lt=Agriculture}}, and {{ill|sanitation|en|sanitation|WD=Q949149|lt=sanitation}}, and will bring success in fishing trips, fertility, motherhood, and easy childbirth.<ref name=Schumacher>{{cite web|last=Schumacher|first=Mark|title=Suijin|url=http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html|publisher=www.onmarkproductions.com|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> [[Shrine]]s devoted to the worship of the Water God are called Suitengū Shrines.
There are several numbers of shrines in Japan associated with deities. An example of this is the Horse God in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka}}, the main shrine for all Suitengū Shrines in Japan. This shrine is visited by pregnant women who wish to ensure a safe and easy birth for their children. Another famous Suitengū Shrine is the {{ill|Tsukiji|en|Tsukiji|WD=Q1201337|lt=Tsukiji}} [[Suitengū (Tokyo)|Suijin Shrine]], located near the {{ill|Tsukiji fish market|en|Tsukiji fish market|WD=Q859471|lt=Tsukiji fish market}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, which was built to protect and watch over the fishermen and their businesses.<ref name=Schumacher />
Suijin is widely worshipped at Suitengū Shrines throughout Japan through {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=votive stone markers}} devoted to the Water God. Most of these stone markers can be found enshrined at {{ill|ditch|en|ditch|WD=Q2048319|lt=dikes}}, agricultural irrigation canals, [[:en:Rice paddy|Rice paddy]] fields, mountain {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs}}, regular springs, streams, rivers, wells, household wells, and even inside sewage water and [[:en:septic tanks|septic tanks]]. When a Suijin stone marker is enshrined at mountain springs that form sources for agricultural waterways, it is often labelled as "water distributing god" (kumari no kami 水分神), in which case it may also be associated with the god of the mountain (Yama no kami 山の神).<ref name=Schumacher />
=== Festivals ===
There are many Japanese festivals dedicated to the water god, most of which occur during summer and winter, though more emphasis is placed on summer festivals. The majority of summer festivals occur at the [[Yasaka Shrine]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and the [[Tsushima Shrine]] in {{ill|Tsushima, Aichi|en|Tsushima, Aichi|WD=Q816736|lt=Tsushima}}. These observances have a significant role in the {{ill|Exorcism|en|Exorcism|WD=Q172663|lt=Exorcism}}<ref>Yen, A. (1975). "Shang-ssu" Festival and Its Myths in China and Japan. Asian Folklore Studies, 34(2), 45-85. doi:10.2307/1177626</ref> of bad spirits and purification, primarily aimed at dangerous {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic}}s, {{ill|Disease|en|Disease|WD=Q12136|lt=Disease}}s and {{ill|Natural disaster|en|Natural disaster|WD=Q8065|lt=natural and man-made water-related disasters}} which commonly happen during the {{ill|summer|en|summer|WD=Q1313|lt=summer}}.
List of festivals worshipping Suijin (this list may not include all festivals):<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi>{{cite web|last=Higashi|title=Suijin|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=227|publisher=Kokugakuin University|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> * Suijin [[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]], December 1 and June 15 — On these days in Japan, various locations hold the [https://www.japanallover.com/2006/05/suijin-matsuri/ Suijin Matsuri], a Shinto celebration and ceremony to honour the god of water. * Kamakura Matsuri, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}, around February 15–17 — {{ill|Igloo|en|Igloo|WD=Q101805|lt=Igloo}}s (called "Kamakura") are built with a small alcove inside dedicated to Suijin, who is honoured during this festival to ensure good crops in the coming year. This festival is over 400 years old. * Suijin-sai, {{ill|Funabashi|en|Funabashi|WD=Q271417|lt=Funabashi}} Fishing Harbour ({{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba}}), April 3 — A festival to pray for good fishing hauls and safety at sea. All [[:en:fishing boats|fishing boats]] from the area gather in the harbour and a [[Kagura|Kagura ritual]] (sacred Shinto music and dance), dedicated to the god of the sea, is performed onboard each vessel. A ritual existing since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo Period}}, the festival is called Futuna-sai for short. * Suitengū Spring Festival, Kurume City ({{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka}}), May 3–5 — This annual festival, held at the Suitengū Shrine in Kurume City, is reportedly over 800 years old. Participants pray for safe and easy childbirth, for protection from {{ill|Drowning|en|Drowning|WD=Q506616|lt=Drowning}}, and ask for the safety and health of children. This festival is also held at other Suitengū sites in Japan on May 5. Another major festival in Kurume City is the Water Festival, which takes place from August 3 to 5. * Okinohata Suitengu Festival, {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa}} (Fukuoka), May 3–5 — Held on the canal by the shrine for three days and nights, drawing about 30,000 total visitors. Many pray for their children to be protected from drowning. * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]], near {{ill|Tsukuba|en|Tsukuba|WD=Q273863|lt=Tsukuba}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki}}), around July 25 — The Anniversary feast of Suijin-gū Shrine of Tsuchiura City (Ibaraki). Water is splashed over the portable shrine.<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi />
== References == {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://archive.today/20130210114439/http://groups.yahoo.com/group/Darumasan-Japan/message/496 Yahoo groups] *[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/12-devas.shtml On Mark Productions: Twelve Devas] *[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html On Mark Productions: Suijin]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] {{Infobox deity}} {{Short description|Shinto god of water in Japan}}[[File:水神.JPG|thumb|An altar to Suijin, located along the {{ill|Kiso River|en|Kiso River|WD=Q779607|lt=Kiso River|1=Kiso River}}]] {{nihongo|'''Suijin''' or '''Mizugami'''|, すいじん, みずがみ||{{small|lit.}} "water god/deity"}} is a general name for the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=god of water|1=List of water deities}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. The term refers to the heavenly and earthly manifestations of the benevolent [[Shinto]] divinity of water (mainly [[:en:freshwater|freshwater]]), as well as to a wide variety of mythical and magical creatures found in {{ill|lake|en|lake|WD=Q23397|lt=lake|1=Lake}}s, {{ill|Pond|en|Pond|WD=Q12719646|lt=Pond|1=Pond}}s, {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs|1=Spring (hydrology)}}, and {{ill|Well|en|Well|WD=Q43483|lt=Well|1=Well}}s, including serpents ({{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake|1=Snake}}s, {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon|1=Dragon}}s, {{ill|eel|en|eel|WD=Q128685|lt=eel|1=Eel}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish|1=Fish}}, {{ill|Turtle|en|Turtle|WD=Q223044|lt=Turtle|1=Turtle}}s), and the flesh-eating {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=kappa|1=Kappa (folklore)}}. '''Mizu-no-kamisama''', '''Mizugami''', or '''Suijin''' is popularly {{ill|Deference|en|Deference|WD=Q396568|lt=revered|1=Deference}} and {{ill|Worship|en|Worship|WD=Q2514663|lt=worshipped|1=Worship}} in {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=temples|1=Temple}} and continues to influence {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese culture|1=Culture of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Japanese Culture: Everything you need to know {{!}} InsideJapan Tours|url=https://www.insidejapantours.com/japanese-culture/|access-date=2021-01-22|website=www.insidejapantours.com}}</ref> Other names of Suijin include {{nihongo|'''Suiten'''|水天||{{small|lit.}} "water heaven"}} and {{nihongo|'''Suiō'''|水王||{{small|lit.}} "water lord/king"}}.
Suijin is often conflated with [[Ryūjin]], the Japanese dragon god associated with water. [[Acala]] is sometimes called "Suijin" because of his believed association with {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=waterfalls|1=Waterfall}}. Suijin appears as a stone plaque or even a small stone set upright near the emergence of a spring.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=In Handbooks of World Mythology. Santa Barbara, Calif : ABC-CLIO.|isbn=9781576074671|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255 255]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255}}</ref>
==Worship==
The Shinto water god is believed to be the guardian of {{ill|Fisherman|en|Fisherman|WD=Q331432|lt=fishermen|1=Fisherman}} and the {{ill|Patron saint|en|Patron saint|WD=Q28914|lt=Patron saint|1=Patron saint}} of {{ill|Fertility|en|Fertility|WD=Q964401|lt=Fertility|1=Fertility}}, {{ill|motherhood|en|motherhood|WD=Q1956046|lt=motherhood|1=Motherhood}}, and painless {{ill|Childbirth|en|Childbirth|WD=Q34581|lt=Childbirth|1=Childbirth}}. People worship Suijin with offerings, believing that doing so will ensure pure and unpolluted water for drinking, {{ill|Agriculture|en|Agriculture|WD=Q11451|lt=Agriculture|1=Agriculture}}, and {{ill|sanitation|en|sanitation|WD=Q949149|lt=sanitation|1=Sanitation}}, and will bring success in fishing trips, fertility, motherhood, and easy childbirth.<ref name=Schumacher>{{cite web|last=Schumacher|first=Mark|title=Suijin|url=http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html|publisher=www.onmarkproductions.com|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> [[Shrine]]s devoted to the worship of the Water God are called Suitengū Shrines.
There are several numbers of shrines in Japan associated with deities. An example of this is the Horse God in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume|1=Kurume}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, the main shrine for all Suitengū Shrines in Japan. This shrine is visited by pregnant women who wish to ensure a safe and easy birth for their children. Another famous Suitengū Shrine is the {{ill|Tsukiji|en|Tsukiji|WD=Q1201337|lt=Tsukiji|1=Tsukiji}} [[Suitengū (Tokyo)|Suijin Shrine]], located near the {{ill|Tsukiji fish market|en|Tsukiji fish market|WD=Q859471|lt=Tsukiji fish market|1=Tsukiji fish market}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, which was built to protect and watch over the fishermen and their businesses.<ref name=Schumacher />
Suijin is widely worshipped at Suitengū Shrines throughout Japan through {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=votive stone markers|1=Votive offering}} devoted to the Water God. Most of these stone markers can be found enshrined at {{ill|ditch|en|ditch|WD=Q2048319|lt=dikes|1=Ditch}}, agricultural irrigation canals, [[:en:Rice paddy|Rice paddy]] fields, mountain {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs|1=Spring (hydrology)}}, regular springs, streams, rivers, wells, household wells, and even inside sewage water and [[:en:septic tanks|septic tanks]]. When a Suijin stone marker is enshrined at mountain springs that form sources for agricultural waterways, it is often labelled as "water distributing god" (kumari no kami 水分神), in which case it may also be associated with the god of the mountain (Yama no kami 山の神).<ref name=Schumacher />
=== Festivals ===
There are many Japanese festivals dedicated to the water god, most of which occur during summer and winter, though more emphasis is placed on summer festivals. The majority of summer festivals occur at the [[Yasaka Shrine]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} and the [[Tsushima Shrine]] in {{ill|Tsushima, Aichi|en|Tsushima, Aichi|WD=Q816736|lt=Tsushima|1=Tsushima, Aichi}}. These observances have a significant role in the {{ill|Exorcism|en|Exorcism|WD=Q172663|lt=Exorcism|1=Exorcism}}<ref>Yen, A. (1975). "Shang-ssu" Festival and Its Myths in China and Japan. Asian Folklore Studies, 34(2), 45-85. doi:10.2307/1177626</ref> of bad spirits and purification, primarily aimed at dangerous {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}s, {{ill|Disease|en|Disease|WD=Q12136|lt=Disease|1=Disease}}s and {{ill|Natural disaster|en|Natural disaster|WD=Q8065|lt=natural and man-made water-related disasters|1=Natural disaster}} which commonly happen during the {{ill|summer|en|summer|WD=Q1313|lt=summer|1=Summer}}.
List of festivals worshipping Suijin (this list may not include all festivals):<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi>{{cite web|last=Higashi|title=Suijin|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=227|publisher=Kokugakuin University|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> * Suijin [[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]], December 1 and June 15 — On these days in Japan, various locations hold the [https://www.japanallover.com/2006/05/suijin-matsuri/ Suijin Matsuri], a Shinto celebration and ceremony to honour the god of water. * Kamakura Matsuri, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}, around February 15–17 — {{ill|Igloo|en|Igloo|WD=Q101805|lt=Igloo|1=Igloo}}s (called "Kamakura") are built with a small alcove inside dedicated to Suijin, who is honoured during this festival to ensure good crops in the coming year. This festival is over 400 years old. * Suijin-sai, {{ill|Funabashi|en|Funabashi|WD=Q271417|lt=Funabashi|1=Funabashi}} Fishing Harbour ({{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba|1=Chiba Prefecture}}), April 3 — A festival to pray for good fishing hauls and safety at sea. All [[:en:fishing boats|fishing boats]] from the area gather in the harbour and a [[Kagura|Kagura ritual]] (sacred Shinto music and dance), dedicated to the god of the sea, is performed onboard each vessel. A ritual existing since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo Period|1=Edo period}}, the festival is called Futuna-sai for short. * Suitengū Spring Festival, Kurume City ({{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}), May 3–5 — This annual festival, held at the Suitengū Shrine in Kurume City, is reportedly over 800 years old. Participants pray for safe and easy childbirth, for protection from {{ill|Drowning|en|Drowning|WD=Q506616|lt=Drowning|1=Drowning}}, and ask for the safety and health of children. This festival is also held at other Suitengū sites in Japan on May 5. Another major festival in Kurume City is the Water Festival, which takes place from August 3 to 5. * Okinohata Suitengu Festival, {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa|1=Yanagawa, Fukuoka}} (Fukuoka), May 3–5 — Held on the canal by the shrine for three days and nights, drawing about 30,000 total visitors. Many pray for their children to be protected from drowning. * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]], near {{ill|Tsukuba|en|Tsukuba|WD=Q273863|lt=Tsukuba|1=Tsukuba}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}), around July 25 — The Anniversary feast of Suijin-gū Shrine of Tsuchiura City (Ibaraki). Water is splashed over the portable shrine.<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi />
== References == {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://archive.today/20130210114439/http://groups.yahoo.com/group/Darumasan-Japan/message/496 Yahoo groups] *[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/12-devas.shtml On Mark Productions: Twelve Devas] *[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html On Mark Productions: Suijin]

Santa Barbara, Calif : ABC-CLIO.|isbn=9781576074671|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255 255]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255}}</ref>

Asian Folklore Studies, 34(2), 45-85. doi:10.2307/1177626</ref> of bad spirits and purification, primarily aimed at dangerous {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}s, {{ill|Disease|en|Disease|WD=Q12136|lt=Disease|1=Disease}}s and {{ill|Natural disaster|en|Natural disaster|WD=Q8065|lt=natural and man-made water-related disasters|1=Natural disaster}} which commonly happen during the {{ill|summer|en|summer|WD=Q1313|lt=summer|1=Summer}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] {{Infobox deity}} {{Short description|Shinto god of water in Japan}}[[File:水神.JPG|thumb|An altar to Suijin, located along the {{ill|Kiso River|en|Kiso River|WD=Q779607|lt=Kiso River|1=Kiso River}}]] {{nihongo|'''Suijin''' or '''Mizugami'''|, すいじん, みずがみ||{{small|lit.}} "water god/deity"}} is a general name for the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=god of water|1=List of water deities}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. The term refers to the heavenly and earthly manifestations of the benevolent [[Shinto]] divinity of water (mainly [[:en:freshwater|freshwater]]), as well as to a wide variety of mythical and magical creatures found in {{ill|lake|en|lake|WD=Q23397|lt=lake|1=Lake}}s, {{ill|Pond|en|Pond|WD=Q12719646|lt=Pond|1=Pond}}s, {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs|1=Spring (hydrology)}}, and {{ill|Well|en|Well|WD=Q43483|lt=Well|1=Well}}s, including serpents ({{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake|1=Snake}}s, {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon|1=Dragon}}s, {{ill|eel|en|eel|WD=Q128685|lt=eel|1=Eel}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish|1=Fish}}, {{ill|Turtle|en|Turtle|WD=Q223044|lt=Turtle|1=Turtle}}s), and the flesh-eating {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=kappa|1=Kappa (folklore)}}. '''Mizu-no-kamisama''', '''Mizugami''', or '''Suijin''' is popularly {{ill|Deference|en|Deference|WD=Q396568|lt=revered|1=Deference}} and {{ill|Worship|en|Worship|WD=Q2514663|lt=worshipped|1=Worship}} in {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=temples|1=Temple}} and continues to influence {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese culture|1=Culture of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Japanese Culture: Everything you need to know {{!}} InsideJapan Tours|url=https://www.insidejapantours.com/japanese-culture/|access-date=2021-01-22|website=www.insidejapantours.com}}</ref> Other names of Suijin include {{nihongo|'''Suiten'''|水天||{{small|lit.}} "water heaven"}} and {{nihongo|'''Suiō'''|水王||{{small|lit.}} "water lord/king"}}.
Suijin is often conflated with [[Ryūjin]], the Japanese dragon god associated with water. [[Acala]] is sometimes called "Suijin" because of his believed association with {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=waterfalls|1=Waterfall}}. Suijin appears as a stone plaque or even a small stone set upright near the emergence of a spring.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=In Handbooks of World Mythology. Santa Barbara, Calif : ABC-CLIO.|isbn=9781576074671|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255 255]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255}}</ref>
==Worship==
The Shinto water god is believed to be the guardian of {{ill|Fisherman|en|Fisherman|WD=Q331432|lt=fishermen|1=Fisherman}} and the {{ill|Patron saint|en|Patron saint|WD=Q28914|lt=Patron saint|1=Patron saint}} of {{ill|Fertility|en|Fertility|WD=Q964401|lt=Fertility|1=Fertility}}, {{ill|motherhood|en|motherhood|WD=Q1956046|lt=motherhood|1=Motherhood}}, and painless {{ill|Childbirth|en|Childbirth|WD=Q34581|lt=Childbirth|1=Childbirth}}. People worship Suijin with offerings, believing that doing so will ensure pure and unpolluted water for drinking, {{ill|Agriculture|en|Agriculture|WD=Q11451|lt=Agriculture|1=Agriculture}}, and {{ill|sanitation|en|sanitation|WD=Q949149|lt=sanitation|1=Sanitation}}, and will bring success in fishing trips, fertility, motherhood, and easy childbirth.<ref name=Schumacher>{{cite web|last=Schumacher|first=Mark|title=Suijin|url=http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html|publisher=www.onmarkproductions.com|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> [[Shrine]]s devoted to the worship of the Water God are called Suitengū Shrines.
There are several numbers of shrines in Japan associated with deities. An example of this is the Horse God in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume|1=Kurume}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, the main shrine for all Suitengū Shrines in Japan. This shrine is visited by pregnant women who wish to ensure a safe and easy birth for their children. Another famous Suitengū Shrine is the {{ill|Tsukiji|en|Tsukiji|WD=Q1201337|lt=Tsukiji|1=Tsukiji}} [[Suitengū (Tokyo)|Suijin Shrine]], located near the {{ill|Tsukiji fish market|en|Tsukiji fish market|WD=Q859471|lt=Tsukiji fish market|1=Tsukiji fish market}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, which was built to protect and watch over the fishermen and their businesses.<ref name=Schumacher />
Suijin is widely worshipped at Suitengū Shrines throughout Japan through {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=votive stone markers|1=Votive offering}} devoted to the Water God. Most of these stone markers can be found enshrined at {{ill|ditch|en|ditch|WD=Q2048319|lt=dikes|1=Ditch}}, agricultural irrigation canals, [[:en:Rice paddy|Rice paddy]] fields, mountain {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs|1=Spring (hydrology)}}, regular springs, streams, rivers, wells, household wells, and even inside sewage water and [[:en:septic tanks|septic tanks]]. When a Suijin stone marker is enshrined at mountain springs that form sources for agricultural waterways, it is often labelled as "water distributing god" (kumari no kami 水分神), in which case it may also be associated with the god of the mountain (Yama no kami 山の神).<ref name=Schumacher />
=== Festivals ===
There are many Japanese festivals dedicated to the water god, most of which occur during summer and winter, though more emphasis is placed on summer festivals. The majority of summer festivals occur at the [[Yasaka Shrine]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} and the [[Tsushima Shrine]] in {{ill|Tsushima, Aichi|en|Tsushima, Aichi|WD=Q816736|lt=Tsushima|1=Tsushima, Aichi}}. These observances have a significant role in the {{ill|Exorcism|en|Exorcism|WD=Q172663|lt=Exorcism|1=Exorcism}}<ref>Yen, A. (1975). "Shang-ssu" Festival and Its Myths in China and Japan. Asian Folklore Studies, 34(2), 45-85. doi:10.2307/1177626</ref> of bad spirits and purification, primarily aimed at dangerous {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}s, {{ill|Disease|en|Disease|WD=Q12136|lt=Disease|1=Disease}}s and {{ill|Natural disaster|en|Natural disaster|WD=Q8065|lt=natural and man-made water-related disasters|1=Natural disaster}} which commonly happen during the {{ill|summer|en|summer|WD=Q1313|lt=summer|1=Summer}}.
List of festivals worshipping Suijin (this list may not include all festivals):<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi>{{cite web|last=Higashi|title=Suijin|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=227|publisher=Kokugakuin University|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> * Suijin [[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]], December 1 and June 15 — On these days in Japan, various locations hold the [https://www.japanallover.com/2006/05/suijin-matsuri/ Suijin Matsuri], a Shinto celebration and ceremony to honour the god of water. * Kamakura Matsuri, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}, around February 15–17 — {{ill|Igloo|en|Igloo|WD=Q101805|lt=Igloo|1=Igloo}}s (called "Kamakura") are built with a small alcove inside dedicated to Suijin, who is honoured during this festival to ensure good crops in the coming year. This festival is over 400 years old. * Suijin-sai, {{ill|Funabashi|en|Funabashi|WD=Q271417|lt=Funabashi|1=Funabashi}} Fishing Harbour ({{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba|1=Chiba Prefecture}}), April 3 — A festival to pray for good fishing hauls and safety at sea. All [[:en:fishing boats|fishing boats]] from the area gather in the harbour and a [[Kagura|Kagura ritual]] (sacred Shinto music and dance), dedicated to the god of the sea, is performed onboard each vessel. A ritual existing since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo Period|1=Edo period}}, the festival is called Futuna-sai for short. * Suitengū Spring Festival, Kurume City ({{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}), May 3–5 — This annual festival, held at the Suitengū Shrine in Kurume City, is reportedly over 800 years old. Participants pray for safe and easy childbirth, for protection from {{ill|Drowning|en|Drowning|WD=Q506616|lt=Drowning|1=Drowning}}, and ask for the safety and health of children. This festival is also held at other Suitengū sites in Japan on May 5. Another major festival in Kurume City is the Water Festival, which takes place from August 3 to 5. * Okinohata Suitengu Festival, {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa|1=Yanagawa, Fukuoka}} (Fukuoka), May 3–5 — Held on the canal by the shrine for three days and nights, drawing about 30,000 total visitors. Many pray for their children to be protected from drowning. * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]], near {{ill|Tsukuba|en|Tsukuba|WD=Q273863|lt=Tsukuba|1=Tsukuba}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}), around July 25 — The Anniversary feast of Suijin-gū Shrine of Tsuchiura City (Ibaraki). Water is splashed over the portable shrine.<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi />
== References == {{reflist}}
==External links== *[https://archive.today/20130210114439/http://groups.yahoo.com/group/Darumasan-Japan/message/496 Yahoo groups] *[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/12-devas.shtml On Mark Productions: Twelve Devas] *[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html On Mark Productions: Suijin]

Asian Folklore Studies, 34(2), 45-85. doi:10.2307/1177626</ref> of bad spirits and purification, primarily aimed at dangerous {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}s, {{ill|Disease|en|Disease|WD=Q12136|lt=Disease|1=Disease}}s and {{ill|Natural disaster|en|Natural disaster|WD=Q8065|lt=natural and man-made water-related disasters|1=Natural disaster}} which commonly happen during the {{ill|summer|en|summer|WD=Q1313|lt=summer|1=Summer}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin]][[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Suijin]][[es:Suijin]][[fi:Suijin]][[fr:Suijin]][[ja:水神]][[ml:സുയിജിൻ]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯢꯖꯤꯟ]][[nl:Suijin]][[sv:Suijin]][[zh:水神 (日本)]] {{Infobox deity}} {{Short description|Shinto god of water in Japan}}[[File:水神.JPG|thumb|An altar to Suijin, located along the {{ill|Kiso River|en|Kiso River|WD=Q779607|lt=Kiso River|1=Kiso River}}]] {{nihongo|'''Suijin''' or '''Mizugami'''|, すいじん, みずがみ||{{small|lit.}} "water god/deity"}} is a general name for the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|WD=Q13416336|lt=god of water|1=List of water deities}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. The term refers to the heavenly and earthly manifestations of the benevolent [[Shinto]] divinity of water (mainly [[:en:freshwater|freshwater]]), as well as to a wide variety of mythical and magical creatures found in {{ill|lake|en|lake|WD=Q23397|lt=lake|1=Lake}}s, {{ill|Pond|en|Pond|WD=Q12719646|lt=Pond|1=Pond}}s, {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs|1=Spring (hydrology)}}, and {{ill|Well|en|Well|WD=Q43483|lt=Well|1=Well}}s, including serpents ({{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake|1=Snake}}s, {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon|1=Dragon}}s, {{ill|eel|en|eel|WD=Q128685|lt=eel|1=Eel}}s, {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish|1=Fish}}, {{ill|Turtle|en|Turtle|WD=Q223044|lt=Turtle|1=Turtle}}s), and the flesh-eating {{ill|Kappa (folklore)|en|Kappa (folklore)|WD=Q335140|lt=kappa|1=Kappa (folklore)}}. '''Mizu-no-kamisama''', '''Mizugami''', or '''Suijin''' is popularly {{ill|Deference|en|Deference|WD=Q396568|lt=revered|1=Deference}} and {{ill|Worship|en|Worship|WD=Q2514663|lt=worshipped|1=Worship}} in {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=temples|1=Temple}} and continues to influence {{ill|Culture of Japan|en|Culture of Japan|WD=Q831454|lt=Japanese culture|1=Culture of Japan}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Japanese Culture: Everything you need to know {{!}} InsideJapan Tours|url=https://www.insidejapantours.com/japanese-culture/|access-date=2021-01-22|website=www.insidejapantours.com}}</ref> Other names of Suijin include {{nihongo|'''Suiten'''|水天||{{small|lit.}} "water heaven"}} and {{nihongo|'''Suiō'''|水王||{{small|lit.}} "water lord/king"}}.
Suijin is often conflated with [[Ryūjin]], the Japanese dragon god associated with water. [[Acala]] is sometimes called "Suijin" because of his believed association with {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=waterfalls|1=Waterfall}}. Suijin appears as a stone plaque or even a small stone set upright near the emergence of a spring.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|year=2003|publisher=In Handbooks of World Mythology. Santa Barbara, Calif : ABC-CLIO.|isbn=9781576074671|pages=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255 255]|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/255}}</ref>
==Worship==
The Shinto water god is believed to be the guardian of {{ill|Fisherman|en|Fisherman|WD=Q331432|lt=fishermen|1=Fisherman}} and the {{ill|Patron saint|en|Patron saint|WD=Q28914|lt=Patron saint|1=Patron saint}} of {{ill|Fertility|en|Fertility|WD=Q964401|lt=Fertility|1=Fertility}}, {{ill|motherhood|en|motherhood|WD=Q1956046|lt=motherhood|1=Motherhood}}, and painless {{ill|Childbirth|en|Childbirth|WD=Q34581|lt=Childbirth|1=Childbirth}}. People worship Suijin with offerings, believing that doing so will ensure pure and unpolluted water for drinking, {{ill|Agriculture|en|Agriculture|WD=Q11451|lt=Agriculture|1=Agriculture}}, and {{ill|sanitation|en|sanitation|WD=Q949149|lt=sanitation|1=Sanitation}}, and will bring success in fishing trips, fertility, motherhood, and easy childbirth.<ref name=Schumacher>{{cite web|last=Schumacher|first=Mark|title=Suijin|url=http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/suijin.html|publisher=www.onmarkproductions.com|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> [[Shrine]]s devoted to the worship of the Water God are called Suitengū Shrines.
There are several numbers of shrines in Japan associated with deities. An example of this is the Horse God in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume|1=Kurume}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, the main shrine for all Suitengū Shrines in Japan. This shrine is visited by pregnant women who wish to ensure a safe and easy birth for their children. Another famous Suitengū Shrine is the {{ill|Tsukiji|en|Tsukiji|WD=Q1201337|lt=Tsukiji|1=Tsukiji}} [[Suitengū (Tokyo)|Suijin Shrine]], located near the {{ill|Tsukiji fish market|en|Tsukiji fish market|WD=Q859471|lt=Tsukiji fish market|1=Tsukiji fish market}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, which was built to protect and watch over the fishermen and their businesses.<ref name=Schumacher />
Suijin is widely worshipped at Suitengū Shrines throughout Japan through {{ill|Votive offering|en|Votive offering|WD=Q2601788|lt=votive stone markers|1=Votive offering}} devoted to the Water God. Most of these stone markers can be found enshrined at {{ill|ditch|en|ditch|WD=Q2048319|lt=dikes|1=Ditch}}, agricultural irrigation canals, [[:en:Rice paddy|Rice paddy]] fields, mountain {{ill|Spring (hydrology)|en|Spring (hydrology)|WD=Q124714|lt=springs|1=Spring (hydrology)}}, regular springs, streams, rivers, wells, household wells, and even inside sewage water and [[:en:septic tanks|septic tanks]]. When a Suijin stone marker is enshrined at mountain springs that form sources for agricultural waterways, it is often labelled as "water distributing god" (kumari no kami 水分神), in which case it may also be associated with the god of the mountain (Yama no kami 山の神).<ref name=Schumacher />
=== Festivals ===
There are many Japanese festivals dedicated to the water god, most of which occur during summer and winter, though more emphasis is placed on summer festivals. The majority of summer festivals occur at the [[Yasaka Shrine]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} and the [[Tsushima Shrine]] in {{ill|Tsushima, Aichi|en|Tsushima, Aichi|WD=Q816736|lt=Tsushima|1=Tsushima, Aichi}}. These observances have a significant role in the {{ill|Exorcism|en|Exorcism|WD=Q172663|lt=Exorcism|1=Exorcism}}<ref>Yen, A. (1975). "Shang-ssu" Festival and Its Myths in China and Japan. Asian Folklore Studies, 34(2), 45-85. doi:10.2307/1177626</ref> of bad spirits and purification, primarily aimed at dangerous {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}s, {{ill|Disease|en|Disease|WD=Q12136|lt=Disease|1=Disease}}s and {{ill|Natural disaster|en|Natural disaster|WD=Q8065|lt=natural and man-made water-related disasters|1=Natural disaster}} which commonly happen during the {{ill|summer|en|summer|WD=Q1313|lt=summer|1=Summer}}.
List of festivals worshipping Suijin (this list may not include all festivals):<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi>{{cite web|last=Higashi|title=Suijin|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=227|publisher=Kokugakuin University|access-date=2014-02-28}}</ref> * Suijin [[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]], December 1 and June 15 — On these days in Japan, various locations hold the [https://www.japanallover.com/2006/05/suijin-matsuri/ Suijin Matsuri], a Shinto celebration and ceremony to honour the god of water. * Kamakura Matsuri, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}, around February 15–17 — {{ill|Igloo|en|Igloo|WD=Q101805|lt=Igloo|1=Igloo}}s (called "Kamakura") are built with a small alcove inside dedicated to Suijin, who is honoured during this festival to ensure good crops in the coming year. This festival is over 400 years old. * Suijin-sai, {{ill|Funabashi|en|Funabashi|WD=Q271417|lt=Funabashi|1=Funabashi}} Fishing Harbour ({{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba|1=Chiba Prefecture}}), April 3 — A festival to pray for good fishing hauls and safety at sea. All [[:en:fishing boats|fishing boats]] from the area gather in the harbour and a [[Kagura|Kagura ritual]] (sacred Shinto music and dance), dedicated to the god of the sea, is performed onboard each vessel. A ritual existing since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo Period|1=Edo period}}, the festival is called Futuna-sai for short. * Suitengū Spring Festival, Kurume City ({{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}), May 3–5 — This annual festival, held at the Suitengū Shrine in Kurume City, is reportedly over 800 years old. Participants pray for safe and easy childbirth, for protection from {{ill|Drowning|en|Drowning|WD=Q506616|lt=Drowning|1=Drowning}}, and ask for the safety and health of children. This festival is also held at other Suitengū sites in Japan on May 5. Another major festival in Kurume City is the Water Festival, which takes place from August 3 to 5. * Okinohata Suitengu Festival, {{ill|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|en|Yanagawa, Fukuoka|WD=Q585630|lt=Yanagawa|1=Yanagawa, Fukuoka}} (Fukuoka), May 3–5 — Held on the canal by the shrine for three days and nights, drawing about 30,000 total visitors. Many pray for their children to be protected from drowning. * [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]], near {{ill|Tsukuba|en|Tsukuba|WD=Q273863|lt=Tsukuba|1=Tsukuba}} ({{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|WD=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}), around July 25 — The Anniversary feast of Suijin-gū Shrine of Tsuchiura City (Ibaraki). Water is splashed over the portable shrine.<ref name=Schumacher /><ref name=Higashi />
